items.prompt_md: In the dynamic window used for "longest substring without repeating characters," what actually causes the **left edge** to advance?
options: The right edge reaches the end of the string
The window size exceeds some fixed limit, like `k`
The incoming character's last-seen index falls inside the current window, breaking uniqueness
The left edge advances by exactly one position every time the right edge moves
items.answer: The incoming character's last-seen index falls inside the current window, breaking uniqueness
items.explanation_md: The left edge is *conditional*, not scheduled. It only jumps forward when the map reveals the window just broke its constraint — here, that a character showed up twice inside the current bounds.

items.prompt_md: What job does the hash map (e.g. `last_seen: char -> index`) do in the dynamic-window algorithm?
options: It stores every substring seen so far so they can be compared at the end
It gives an O(1) answer to "is this character currently inside my window, and where"
It counts how many total windows were evaluated
It caches the final answer so the function can be called again cheaply
items.answer: It gives an O(1) answer to "is this character currently inside my window, and where"
items.explanation_md: Without the map you'd have to rescan the window to check membership, which is exactly the O(n) per-step cost the map exists to avoid.

items.prompt_md: The dynamic window has a nested-looking structure — an outer loop advancing `right`, with a shrink step (sometimes a `while`) advancing `left`. Why is the total time still O(n) and not O(n^2)?
options: Hash map lookups are free, so only the outer loop counts toward complexity
The shrink loop only ever runs once in total, no matter the input
Each index is entered by `right` at most once and exited by `left` at most once, so total pointer movement across the whole scan is bounded by 2n
The Python interpreter automatically collapses nested loops into a single pass
items.answer: Each index is entered by `right` at most once and exited by `left` at most once, so total pointer movement across the whole scan is bounded by 2n
items.explanation_md: It looks like nested loops, but `left` never resets and never moves backward — across the entire run it takes at most n steps forward, same as `right`. Two bounded-by-n walks add to O(n), not O(n) times O(n).

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the hash map that tracks the window's contents in the longest-substring-without-repeats algorithm?
options: O(n^2), one entry per possible substring
O(1), because the map object is reused across iterations
O(min(n, alphabet size)), since the map holds at most one entry per distinct character seen so far
O(log n), because the map is implemented as a balanced tree
items.answer: O(min(n, alphabet size)), since the map holds at most one entry per distinct character seen so far
items.explanation_md: The map can never have more entries than there are distinct characters possible, and never more than the string's length either — whichever is smaller bounds it.

items.prompt_md: Why does a dynamic-window shrink step need a `while` loop rather than a single `if` check, in general?
options: It doesn't — `while` and `if` behave identically here, `while` is just convention
If the window is still invalid after shrinking once, it must keep shrinking until valid again; a single `if` would leave it broken
`while` loops execute faster than `if` statements in Python
The window can only ever become invalid once per right-pointer step
items.answer: If the window is still invalid after shrinking once, it must keep shrinking until valid again; a single `if` would leave it broken
items.explanation_md: One shrink step may not be enough — think of minimum-window-substring, where several characters' counts might need draining before the window becomes valid (or invalid) again. `while` guarantees the window is fully corrected before moving on.

items.prompt_md: A buggy implementation updates `best = max(best, right - left + 1)` *before* checking whether the incoming character duplicates one already in the window. What's the effect?
options: No effect — the order of these two steps doesn't matter
The recorded length can include a window that momentarily violated the constraint, before it gets shrunk back to valid
It causes an infinite loop
It changes the algorithm's time complexity from O(n) to O(n^2)
items.answer: The recorded length can include a window that momentarily violated the constraint, before it gets shrunk back to valid
items.explanation_md: The answer must only be updated once the window is known-valid. Recording it before the shrink step means you might record a window that actually contains a duplicate.

items.prompt_md: In a dynamic window tracking per-character counts (e.g. "at most k distinct characters"), what bug results from forgetting to decrement a character's count when the left edge passes over it?
options: The map will keep reporting that character as "inside" the window even after it left, corrupting future validity checks
The algorithm crashes with a key-not-found error
The window will never be able to shrink at all
Nothing — only the character at the new right edge matters for validity
items.answer: The map will keep reporting that character as "inside" the window even after it left, corrupting future validity checks
items.explanation_md: The window map has to mirror what's truly between `left` and `right`. Skipping the decrement leaves stale counts behind, so the validity check starts lying.

items.prompt_md: How does this dynamic (variable-size) window differ from the fixed-size window from the previous lesson?
options: The dynamic window never needs a hash map
The fixed window's left edge follows a strict, unconditional rule (e.g. one step per right-edge step); the dynamic window's left edge only moves when a constraint is violated
The dynamic window only applies to arrays, never strings
The fixed window is O(n^2); the dynamic window is O(n)
items.answer: The fixed window's left edge follows a strict, unconditional rule (e.g. one step per right-edge step); the dynamic window's left edge only moves when a constraint is violated
items.explanation_md: Both edges move in both variants — the difference is *why* the left edge moves. Fixed-size: on a schedule. Dynamic: reactively, whenever the window's state stops satisfying the problem's condition.

items.prompt_md: For "minimum window substring" (smallest window containing every character of a target), when is it correct to try shrinking from the left?
options: Only after the right pointer has reached the end of the string
Whenever the window currently satisfies the constraint — shrink while it's still valid, recording the best answer along the way, and stop the moment it becomes invalid
Never — minimum-window problems only ever grow the window
Only when the current window size is an even number
items.answer: Whenever the window currently satisfies the constraint — shrink while it's still valid, recording the best answer along the way, and stop the moment it becomes invalid
items.explanation_md: This is the mirror image of the longest-window shrink rule: here you shrink *while valid* (to find the smallest valid window), not *while invalid*. The answer gets updated during the valid shrink, not after.

items.prompt_md: The lesson's trigger phrasing is "longest/shortest substring or subarray such that some condition holds continuously." What should that phrasing cue you to reach for?
options: A fixed-size window that slides forward by exactly one element each step
Sort the array first, then scan once
A dynamic window: grow the right edge, and shrink the left edge with a hash map whenever the condition breaks
Binary search over the answer's magnitude
items.answer: A dynamic window: grow the right edge, and shrink the left edge with a hash map whenever the condition breaks
items.explanation_md: "Longest/shortest ... such that a condition holds continuously" over a contiguous range is the dynamic-window signature — the window size isn't fixed, it responds to whether the condition currently holds.

items.prompt_md: Why is checking "is this character currently inside the window" via a hash map faster than rescanning the window's contents directly?
options: Rescanning costs O(window size) per check, which could make the whole algorithm O(n^2) in the worst case; the hash map makes that check O(1)
Rescanning is just as fast in practice, since windows stay small
Hash maps are always O(1) regardless of what they're compared against, so the comparison is moot
The window's contents are kept sorted, so both approaches are O(log n)
items.answer: Rescanning costs O(window size) per check, which could make the whole algorithm O(n^2) in the worst case; the hash map makes that check O(1)
items.explanation_md: If you rescanned the window on every right-pointer step, the window could be up to length n, turning an O(n)-step scan into O(n) work per step — O(n^2) overall. The map collapses that check to O(1).

items.prompt_md: You're scanning a string and need the length of the longest run where no character repeats.
options: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: "Longest contiguous run with no repeats" is the classic dynamic-window trigger: grow, and shrink from the left only when a duplicate breaks the constraint.

items.prompt_md: Given a string and a target string, find the smallest window in the first string that contains every character of the target, at least as many times as it appears there.
options: Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: "Smallest window covering a target" is a dynamic window too — grow until valid, then shrink from the left while still valid to squeeze out the minimum size.

items.prompt_md: You have a binary array and a budget k: you may flip at most k zeros to ones. Find the longest run of ones you could end up with.
options: Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: "Longest contiguous run with at-most-k of something" (here, at most k zeros) is exactly the at-most-k dynamic-window shape: track the zero count, shrink while it exceeds k.

items.prompt_md: Find the length of the longest substring that contains at most two distinct characters.
options: Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: "At most k distinct characters, longest substring" is a direct extension of the lesson's follow-up question — track distinct-character counts in a map, shrink while the count exceeds two.

items.prompt_md: You have an array of positive integers and a target sum. Find the length of the smallest contiguous subarray whose sum is at least the target.
options: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: Because every element is positive, the running sum only grows as the window grows and only shrinks as the window shrinks — so shrinking the left edge while the sum still meets the target is safe and monotonic, the dynamic-window signature.

items.prompt_md: Given an array that may contain negative numbers, determine whether any contiguous subarray sums to exactly a target value k.
options: Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
items.answer: Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
items.explanation_md: Trap: with negative numbers present, growing the window can decrease the sum, so shrinking the left edge is no longer a monotonic, safe move — the dynamic-window shrink rule breaks. Prefix sums stored in a hash map (look for `prefix - k`) handle negatives correctly.

items.prompt_md: Given an array sorted in ascending order, find two numbers that add up to a target value.
options: Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
items.answer: Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
items.explanation_md: Trap: this isn't a contiguous-substring/subarray problem at all — there's no window to grow or shrink. Sortedness lets you reason about a pair with pointers converging from both ends instead.

items.prompt_md: Find the length of the longest substring where, after replacing at most k characters, every character in the window could be made identical.
options: Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: "Longest window with at most k characters that don't match the majority" is an at-most-k dynamic window: track character counts, shrink while (window size - most frequent count) exceeds k.

items.prompt_md: Walking along a row of fruit trees, each tree has one type of fruit. You carry exactly two baskets, each holding only one fruit type. Find the maximum number of contiguous trees you can collect from.
options: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: Under the disguise, this is "longest contiguous run with at most two distinct types" — the same at-most-k-distinct dynamic window as before.

items.prompt_md: You have a binary array and may flip at most one zero to a one. Find the length of the longest contiguous run of ones achievable.
options: Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: Same at-most-k shape with k = 1: track the zero count in the window, shrink from the left the moment it exceeds one.

items.prompt_md: Find the length of the longest contiguous subarray that contains at most k distinct integers.
options: Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
items.answer: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
items.explanation_md: "Longest contiguous subarray with at most k distinct values" is the canonical dynamic-window-with-a-counter problem: grow, and shrink while the distinct count exceeds k.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array that may include negative integers, count how many contiguous subarrays sum to exactly zero.
options: Grow a window and shrink it from the left while it's invalid, tracking counts in a hash map
Use two pointers starting at both ends of a sorted array and move them inward
Use a fixed-size window that slides forward one step at a time
Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
items.answer: Compute prefix sums and look each one up in a hash map
items.explanation_md: Trap: negative values mean the window's sum isn't monotonic as it grows or shrinks, so there's no valid shrink rule to exploit. Track running prefix sums in a hash map and count repeats — that's what a zero-sum subarray means (two equal prefix sums).
